---
title: "Negative Numbers — Below the Ground"
subtitle: "What lives to the left of zero, and how to compute with it"
lesson-id: "t1.negative-numbers"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.number-line", "t1.add-subtract"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["systems", "research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 2 — The Number Line](../02-number-line/index.qmd)
   and [Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](../04-add-subtract/index.qmd)?
2. When you saw $5 - 12$ in Lesson 4 and got $-7$, did that feel
   reasonable, or strange?
3. Have you ever seen a thermometer dip below zero, or a bank account go
   into overdraft?
:::

## Touch

Set the **starting position** and the **operation**. The marker shows
where you land.

```{ojs}
viewof startA = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Start at:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof op = Inputs.select(["+ 5", "+ 2", "− 5", "− 8", "− (−3)", "× (−1)"], {value: "− 8", label: "Apply:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  // compute result
  let result;
  switch(op) {
    case "+ 5": result = startA + 5; break;
    case "+ 2": result = startA + 2; break;
    case "− 5": result = startA - 5; break;
    case "− 8": result = startA - 8; break;
    case "− (−3)": result = startA - (-3); break;
    case "× (−1)": result = startA * -1; break;
  }
  const W = 720, H = 130, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -15, xMax = 15;
  const x = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const ticks = d3.range(xMin, xMax + 1, 5);

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad)
    .attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  for (const t of ticks) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(t)).attr("x2", x(t))
      .attr("y1", H/2 - 6).attr("y2", H/2 + 6)
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 1.4);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x(t)).attr("y", H/2 + 24)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13")
      .attr("fill", t === 0 ? "#c2185b" : "#1f2933")
      .attr("font-weight", t === 0 ? "bold" : "normal")
      .text(t);
  }
  // start marker
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(startA)).attr("cy", H/2 + 18).attr("r", 6)
    .attr("fill", "#9e9e9e");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(startA)).attr("y", H/2 + 50)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`start: ${startA}`);
  // result marker
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(result)).attr("cy", H/2 - 18).attr("r", 8)
    .attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(result)).attr("y", H/2 - 28)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`${result}`);
  // arc connecting them
  const xf = x(startA), xt = x(result);
  const mid = (xf + xt) / 2;
  svg.append("path")
    .attr("d", `M ${xf},${H/2 + 14} Q ${mid},${H/2 + 35} ${xt},${H/2 - 12}`)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-width", 1)
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "3 3");
  return svg.node();
}
```

```{ojs}
{
  let result;
  switch(op) {
    case "+ 5": result = startA + 5; break;
    case "+ 2": result = startA + 2; break;
    case "− 5": result = startA - 5; break;
    case "− 8": result = startA - 8; break;
    case "− (−3)": result = startA - (-3); break;
    case "× (−1)": result = startA * -1; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; font-family:monospace; font-size:1.2rem; margin-top:0.5rem; font-weight:bold;">
    ${startA} ${op} = ${result}
  </div>`;
}
```

Try the operation **− (−3)**. Subtracting a negative *moves you to the
right*. That's the strangest rule in negative-number arithmetic — and
the line shows you why.

## Wonder

1. Why is **3 − 5 = −2** but **5 − 3 = 2**? What changes when you
   reverse the order?
2. What does **− (−3)** mean? Why might subtracting a negative have the
   *opposite* effect of subtracting a positive?
3. What is **(−1) × (−1)**? (Most learners are taught this is **+1** but
   never *shown why*.)

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute, by walking the number line:
$$4 - 7, \quad -3 - 5, \quad -8 + 11.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same rule as before: $-$ hops left, $+$ hops right. The starting point
or the result can be negative.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 - 7 = -3$ (start at 4, hop left 7).
$-3 - 5 = -8$ (start at −3, hop left 5).
$-8 + 11 = 3$ (start at −8, hop right 11 — past zero into the positives).

The number line did all the work. Negative numbers are *not* a special
case — they are just positions to the left of zero.
</details>

### Problem 2

A diver is **5 metres above the surface** (call this $+5$). She dives
down **8 metres**. Where is she relative to the surface?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Down" means subtract. $5 - 8 = ?$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 - 8 = -3$. **3 metres below the surface.** The negative sign records
the direction: below, debt, deficit, before zero — context picks the
meaning.
</details>

### Problem 3

A bank account is overdrawn by **Rs. 200**. Write the balance as a
signed number. After a deposit of **Rs. 350**, what is the new balance?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Old balance: $-200$. After deposit: $-200 + 350 = +150$. The account is
now in the black, with Rs. 150.

The negative number was useful *because* it carried the sign. Banks
quote your balance directly with a "−" or "(OD)" marker. The arithmetic
is exactly the same as for positive numbers.
</details>

### Problem 4

Compute **5 − (−3)**. Then compute **5 + 3**. What do you notice?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Subtract" means hop left. "Subtract a negative" reverses that.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 - (-3) = 8$. And $5 + 3 = 8$. **They are equal.**

The rule: **subtracting a negative is the same as adding the positive.**
$$a - (-b) = a + b.$$

A picture: subtracting "$-3$" means "remove 3 of the *negative* stuff",
which is the same as "add 3 of the *positive* stuff". Or, on the line:
"hop left by $-3$" reverses the hop direction (left becomes right) and
moves you 3 to the right.

This is the rule that lets us reduce *all* subtraction to addition: just
add the **opposite** of the second number. Computers compute subtraction
this way internally.
</details>

### Problem 5

What is **(−1) × (−1)**? You should get $+1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This one needs a *consistency* argument, not a picture argument. Think
about what $(-1) \times 0$ has to be, and split $0 = 1 + (-1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
We know $(-1) \times 0 = 0$ (multiplying by zero gives zero, Lesson 5).
And $0 = 1 + (-1)$. So
$$0 = (-1) \times 0 = (-1) \times (1 + (-1)) = (-1) \times 1 + (-1) \times (-1).$$
Now $(-1) \times 1 = -1$ (multiplying by 1 leaves things unchanged in
sign, Lesson 5). So:
$$0 = -1 + (-1) \times (-1) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (-1) \times (-1) = +1.$$

This is forced — not an arbitrary rule. To keep the distributive law
working, $(-1) \times (-1)$ *must* equal $+1$. The general rule:

- Positive × positive = positive.
- Positive × negative = negative.
- **Negative × negative = positive.**

This is one of those facts that everyone "knows" but few are *shown why*.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **negative number** is a number to the left of zero on the number line:
$$\ldots, -4, -3, -2, -1, \ 0, \ 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$
The whole numbers (positive, negative, and zero) are the **integers**,
written $\mathbb{Z}$.

For any number $a$, the **negation** $-a$ is its mirror across zero. The
sum $a + (-a) = 0$ — they cancel.

**Subtraction is addition of the opposite:** $a - b = a + (-b)$.

**Sign rules for multiplication:**
$$\text{(+)} \times \text{(+)} = \text{(+)}, \quad \text{(+)} \times \text{(−)} = \text{(−)}, \quad \text{(−)} \times \text{(−)} = \text{(+)}.$$

:::

> *Mirror across zero — that is negation;*
> *Subtract a negative — that is addition of its opposite;*
> *Two negatives multiplied — they cancel back to positive.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $-7 + 4 - (-3) - 8$.

A competent solver rewrites every subtraction as addition of the opposite,
then groups:

1. $-7 + 4 - (-3) - 8 = -7 + 4 + 3 + (-8)$.
2. Group the positives and negatives:
   - Positives: $4 + 3 = 7$.
   - Negatives: $-7 + (-8) = -15$.
3. Combine: $7 + (-15) = -8$.

**Answer: −8.** Once subtractions are converted to additions of opposites,
order doesn't matter — addition is commutative for *all* numbers, including
negatives.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute: $-3 + 8$, $\ 6 - 9$, $\ -4 - 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5$, $\ -3$, $\ -9$.
</details>

**W2.** Compute: $7 - (-2)$, $\ -5 + (-3)$, $\ -10 - (-4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$9$, $\ -8$, $\ -6$. Adding a negative is the same as subtracting;
subtracting a negative is the same as adding.
</details>

**W3.** Compute: $(-3) \times 4$, $\ (-2) \times (-7)$, $\ (-1) \times
(-1) \times (-1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-12$, $\ 14$, $\ -1$.

For the third: an odd number of negatives gives negative; an even number
of negatives gives positive. Pair them up and the rest decides the sign.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A submarine is at depth **−45 m** (45 metres below the surface).
It rises **18 m**. What is its new depth?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-45 + 18 = -27$. **27 metres below the surface.**
</details>

**P2.** The temperature on Monday morning is **−4 °C**. By afternoon it
has risen by **9 °C**. By evening it has fallen by **6 °C**. What is the
evening temperature?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Morning: $-4$. Afternoon: $-4 + 9 = 5$. Evening: $5 - 6 = -1$. **−1 °C.**

Three hops on the line — start cold, warm up, cool down a little.
</details>

**P3.** Compute: $\dfrac{(-12) \times 5}{-3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Numerator: negative × positive = negative. Then negative ÷ negative.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(-12) \times 5 = -60$. Then $-60 \div -3 = +20$. **20.**

The same sign rules apply to division: same signs → positive, opposite
signs → negative. (Division is just multiplication by a reciprocal —
Lesson 7.)
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A computer stores integers using a fixed number of bits. With 8
bits, it can store $2^8 = 256$ distinct values. *Signed* 8-bit integers
typically run from **−128 to +127**. What happens when you compute
**127 + 1**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The value tries to become 128 — but 128 isn't representable. What value
*can* the computer store, and what is its sign?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**The result wraps around to −128.** The bit pattern just below 128
(in "two's complement" representation, the standard scheme) is the same
as the bit pattern for −128, so the value flips sign.

This is **signed integer overflow** — distinct from the unsigned
overflow we met in [Lesson 4 Stretch S2](../04-add-subtract/index.qmd).
The infamous *Civilization* "Gandhi nuclear aggression" bug arose from
this exact mechanism: Gandhi's aggression rating, set to a small positive
value and decreased by Indian democracy, underflowed to a very large
positive value (or in some accounts, wrapped from −1 to a huge unsigned
number).

**Sign-aware integer arithmetic on real hardware is a leaky abstraction.**
Understanding negatives as wrapping behaviour is the entry point to
careful systems programming.
</details>

**S2.** ML models often use a **signed loss** that can be positive or
negative — but typically losses are non-negative (squared error,
cross-entropy). When can a loss become negative? Why might that be a
warning sign?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Some losses (rewards in RL, or negative log-likelihoods of probabilities
> 1) can go negative. What does a negative reward signal?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
In **reinforcement learning**, "loss" and "reward" are often signed: a
positive reward signals a good action, a negative reward signals a bad
one. Negative rewards are a *normal* part of training — the model learns
to *avoid* states that produce them.

In **supervised learning**, however, loss should be non-negative. A
negative cross-entropy loss usually signals a bug — typically a
probability greater than 1 (impossible) or a sign error in the loss
formula. Practising with negatives makes spotting these errors second
nature.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Two negatives make a negative."** No — for *multiplication and
   division*, two negatives cancel to a positive ($-3 \times -2 = 6$).
   For *addition and subtraction*, two negatives stay negative ($-3 + -2
   = -5$). Operation matters.
2. **"Negative numbers are smaller than positives — but I can't tell
   which negative is bigger."** $-2$ is *bigger* than $-7$ (further
   right on the line). The minus sign flips your intuition; the line
   restores it.
3. **"$-x$ is always negative."** False. $-x$ is the *opposite* of $x$.
   If $x = -5$, then $-x = 5$ (positive). The minus sign means
   "negation", not "negative".
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive **$(-1) \times (-1) = +1$** from the distributive
   law and $(-1) \times 0 = 0$, without notes?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $-3 + 7 - (-4) - 9$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 11 — Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (signed overflow) — that's a topic many
  programmers don't really understand.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P4 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 2 — The Number Line](../02-number-line/index.qmd)** —
  negatives are positions to the left of zero. The line is the picture.
- **[Lesson 4 — Addition & Subtraction](../04-add-subtract/index.qmd)** —
  the operations themselves are the same; we just allow the operands to
  go below zero.

### What this unlocks

- **[Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** — once we have
  negative numbers, equations like $x + 5 = 2$ have *solutions*
  ($x = -3$), where before they did not.
- **Tier 2 — Linear equations** — every line on a graph requires
  negatives to describe direction.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** Signed integer overflow (*S1*) is one of the most
notorious sources of real-world bugs. The Patriot missile failure of
1991 (a tracking timer overflow), the Ariane 5 rocket explosion (an
unsigned-to-signed cast that overflowed), and many security
vulnerabilities trace back to misunderstood signed arithmetic.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Extending the natural numbers to include
negatives gives the **integers** $\mathbb{Z}$ — the second great
number-system extension. The integers are the prototypical example of a
**ring** in abstract algebra (Tier 6).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Gradients, signed errors, RL rewards,
contrastive losses — every training signal carries a sign. The
direction of weight update *is* the sign of the gradient.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Temperature, elevation (above/below sea level),
banking (credit/debit), accounting (assets/liabilities), time
(BCE/CE), velocity (forward/backward) — wherever there is a
"reference zero", negatives express the other side.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the operation widget makes
  *subtracting a negative* visible as a sign-flip), Boole (the *(−1) × (−1)*
  question is exactly the question Boole would have asked — "why?"),
  Russian school (the consistency argument in P5 is *not* hand-waved
  away), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — and Brahmagupta in 628 CE
  was the first to write down the rules of negative arithmetic
  systematically, including the rule for negative × negative).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
